text: Hi all, I'm Thanh and I'm working on combining the power of as-code and no-code approaches to apply in data engineering and data analytics. I and my team are working on a new DSL and the toolset around it to realize this goal. Outside of this, I'm also quite interested in personal knowledge management and other ways to augment human cognitive abilities. Really looking forward to being part of this community!

text: I have these two people on my shoulder. One says visual programming should be so intuitive, you don't even need documentation. You just point someone to it and beyond a few commands, they just get it and can use it. The other is what you say, it should be esoteric (but not intentionally obtuse) in such a way to drop one's guard and as an invitation to _play_.

text: On a similar note to what <@U01JNTE35QS> mentioned, I think their appeal also comes in the form of the "progressive disclosure of complexity": that is, for a user who just wants to use the tool to manipulate data (without even using formulas, ranges, etc), it's possible to do so very easily, but for the advanced user who wants to create macros, pivot tables, and even full-fledged GUI construction (if we're talking about Excel and VBA), the tool offers little to no "resistance". What's so intriguing is that it does this in a nearly "modeless" way (or at least the modes that do exist are relegated to a few relatively transient contexts): that is to say, there's no explicit "beginner mode" or "advanced mode" in Excel, it's all just _there_, out in the open. Another notable principle is that the abstractions that exist in the app aren't particularly "leaky" (unless we're talking about pivot tables, or the things that <@UA14TGLTC> mentioned, in which case, _oh boy,_ haha), but this is certainly not the primary draw.

text:
Two resources:
1. Bonnie Nardi's "A Small Matter of Programming" is largely a case-study of spreadsheets and CAD systems, which Nardi identifies as the two most successful (by far) end-user programming systems.
2. <@U5TCAFTD3> started a nice Twitter thread about this: <https://twitter.com/stevekrouse/status/1076979937440161793>.

attachments.fallback:
<https://twitter.com/stevekrouse|@stevekrouse>: Key features to steal from spreadsheets:

:ballot_box_with_check: show data, no hidden state
:ballot_box_with_check: no control flow (only expressions, no statements)
:ballot_box_with_check: one mode (no "running" or "stopping")
:ballot_box_with_check: errors isolated (to buggy cells; clean cells unaffected)

started a nice Twitter thread about this:

text:
I think there are some experiments you could repro and find
how spreadsheets can be objectively 10-100x+ better
programming UI experience than traditional linear documents
for certain use cases.

First there's nothing you can do in a spreadsheet language
that you can't do in a linear language, and vice versa, in
terms of functionality.

The only difference is in terms of User Experience, and the
time it takes to build things in one environment vs the
other.

A very common operation is to work with tables of data.
Making easily parseable tables of data is dead simple in
Excel, very difficult in linear langs. In the latter you
have really primitive tools for filling, selection, etc. It
is easy to misalign things. You don't get good secondary
notations (conditional highlighting in excel, for example),
which can be great visual aids. And you often have to insert
noisy delimiters like commas, and worry about escapes. You
are definitely talking about on the order of at least a 10x
speed up in user time to do these kinds of things, and more
realistically on the order of 100x.

Not just for tables, though. The information density of
spreadsheets can be really high (you can arrange your
programs into tiles, tabs, kanban boards, gantt charts, etc,
not just a single linear sequence of token). Massively more
combinatorics to make a visual DSL that fits your problem
domain better.

I could go on. But those 2 UX advantages, one in table
manipulation and one in the visual arrangement of a project,
alone hint at some pretty big non-linear differences in
spreadsheet programming paradigms vs text editor paradigms.
Things that can be numerically and objectively measured and
have orders of magnitude differences, and are not matters of
opinion.

text:
Hi <@U01L1TTFP62>, and welcome here!

I also like the idea about blurring the line between what is textually and visually specified! I like the idea of having an environment where you can mix the two. In fact, I feel this needs when I used Max/MSP long time ago and feel frustrated by nodes/wires programming for some tasks and switching to JavaScript (you can do that with Max/MSP, not easily but you can) was a relief.

I am curious about what you are working on. Do you have any pointers to your work? If you feel so, you can show some of your progress in <#CCL5VVBAN|share-your-work> channel!

text: Hello! I go by iconmaster here on the Internet and I do a lot of research into nontextual programming, object-oriented systems, and tool development. So I thought I would (at the very least) lurk on here and see what people are up to! Here's my GitHub, littered with various attempts at projects in those areas, if that helps introduce me better: <https://github.com/iconmaster5326>

text:
<@UFQQ3P867> Why do you think that is, I mean, that _less understandable_ is a benefit?

One thing I can come up with is that if you're profoundly confused it'll take you into a state of _beginner's mind_ almost automatically, which probably removes some barriers and encourages an open attitude towards learning something new. Whereas something that looks familiar leads you down all kinds of paths that are "obvious" at first sight, but then they aren't, at least not completely, and you end up in this entangled mess of some bits working as you expected and others not at all. Your knowledge gets in the way and nowhere in the process have you been encouraged to open up to something different.

Your knowledge gets in the way and nowhere in the process have you been encouraged to open up to something different.

text:
Hi <@UJ6LDMMN0>! Thanks for the welcome!

Regarding the blurring, I'd say I haven't quite got it figured out myself but feel it's the right thing. I do feel the primary challenge is in deciding what is specified via textual-based input and what is specified via visual-based input and deciding how one transitions from one to the other and back, if at all. One technical challenge in that is visual layout, for either text or visual nodes, and I think that in any case, _automatic_ layout is paramount. Even in text-based languages, I enjoy Lisps and Schemes because the structure yields easy automatic layout and formatting. However, the problem is not easy.

So I have a lot of work to do and mainly a lot of learning. I'm trying to learn more programming language design and implementation and I need to dive into graph data structures and algorithms (I do not have a computer science background).

Another thing I am doing is just building prototypes, and a major challenge in that was choosing a cross-platform GUI language. I really wanted to use F#, but landed on using Racket and its GUI toolkit. I've thought about the distraction of building my on GUI toolkit in F# using Skia because even now I still feel there's no good solution, in any language, for clean and simple development of desktop apps. F# is just so easy to build software that is understandable and works, but Racket's language making capability may come in handy.

Right now, I'm mainly working on prototypes in Racket. My goal is to build a visual language for 2D graphics and animation, similar to Pure Data, vvvv, TouchDesigner, and Processing. I plan to use it to learn the GUI framework and experiment with ideas while constraining the end goal to 2D graphics. After that, I hopefully will have learned enough and will have libraries built up to start working on more general programming environments.

text:
<@U5STGTB3J>

>  One thing I can come up with is that if you're profoundly confused it'll take you into a state of _beginner's mind_ almost automatically, which probably removes some barriers and encourages an open attitude towards learning something new.
This is my feeling also and is also what I was trying to get at with:

>  it should be esoteric (but not intentionally obtuse) in such a way to drop one's guard and as an invitation to _play_.
I think it's a challenging idea to think about what encourages exploration and play more. Is it the right-approach to completely remove any barrier such that any idea is ready deducted from the environment? Or is the right-approach to actually intentionally add in barriers such that they must be broken by the user or that the environment provides an almost plinko like experience?

text: <@U5STGTB3J> honestly it was mostly an intuitive feeling. I agree with your and <@U01L1TTFP62>'s idea that it erases preconceptions for experienced programmers. I also suspect that, similar to how video games challenged UI design, that injecting more fantasy, lore, atmosphere, rule-breaking into the space gives surprising results. VPLs are an opportunity to depart from the platonic grid of text, yet almost immediately we've adopted a set of idioms and norms for this domain as well :yawning_face:

text: Unix/Plan9/GNU famously do have lore all over the place. Maybe it's a cringey nerd thing that continues to gatekeep. But most tools and environments that remain in the imagination of folks have lore to ground them. Enterprise tools are scared of lore unless it's dictated by a marketing team. Indie tools ought to embrace it occasionally

text: Ive been mulling a theory (highly unoriginal one) that everyone has some sort of lore fandom in their life. QAnon and politics are a big one. Sports. Everyone's a nerd about something even if there's a distribution of how obsessive someone can be about it.

text:
Thanks!  I just started a couple weeks ago, although I've had these ideas forever. Wish the GUI toolkit was documented better in Racket, but it is powerful.

>  Does the blocks for circle display the actual circle instance with its properties applied (at least for stroke and fill)?
Yes! That's the idea or hope at least. I think this will end up being a bit of a cross between TouchDesigner and vvvv where I'd like there to be as much information as possible available to display to the user. So even animations should show up. Right now, I'm concentrating on a few nodes mainly to learn the GUI toolkit enough to move towards an "end-to-end" prototype.
